passage: Marie Curie conducted her pioneering research on radioactivity in a small, poorly equipped laboratory in Paris. She worked tirelessly for years[; driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor]. Her dedication ultimately led to her becoming the first person to win two Nobel Prizes in different scientific fields.
stem: Which choice best maintains sentence boundaries?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ; driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor

choices.label: B
choices.text: , driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor

choices.label: C
choices.text: . Driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor

choices.label: D
choices.text: : driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor

explanation: The phrase 'driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor' is a past participial phrase modifying the subject 'She' — it is not an independent clause, because it has no subject and no main verb of its own. A semicolon (Choice A) must join two independent clauses; using it before a participial phrase is a semicolon misuse error. Choice B correctly uses a comma to attach this participial phrase to the independent clause 'She worked tirelessly for years,' which is the standard way to join a modifying participial phrase to the clause it modifies.

distractorRationale.A: Choice A misuses a semicolon. A semicolon is used to join two independent clauses, but 'driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor' is a participial phrase, not an independent clause — it has no subject and no main verb. Placing a semicolon before it violates Standard English conventions.
distractorRationale.B: Choice B is correct. A comma properly attaches the participial phrase to the independent clause it modifies, forming one complete, grammatically correct sentence.
distractorRationale.C: Choice C creates a sentence fragment. Placing a period before 'Driven by intellectual curiosity and an unshakeable commitment to scientific rigor' isolates a participial phrase as a stand-alone sentence, but the phrase has no subject or main verb and cannot stand on its own.
distractorRationale.D: Choice D misuses a colon. A colon can introduce a noun, list, or explanatory clause, but it is not used to attach a participial phrase that modifies the subject of the preceding clause. The participial phrase here is a modifier, not an elaboration introduced by a colon.
